91.098

City of National City
Office of the City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard, National City, CA 92050-4397
George H. Eiser, III—City Attorney                    (619) 336-4220


February 8, 1991


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Formal Written Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

On behalf of National City Mayor George H. Waters, Councilman
Michael R. Dalla, Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer and Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate, formal written advice is hereby requested
pursuant to Government Code §83114(b). I have been authorized to
make this request by action of the City Council taken on January 9,
1991.

I.    FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The City of National City is located in southern San Diego
County. The City has a population of approximately 55,000,
and is 8.55 square miles in area. The City's land area is
approximately 7,734 square miles, or 4,697 acres in area. The
City is bounded by the City of San Diego on the north, the
City of Chula Vista on the south, the Pacific Ocean on the
west, and the County of San Diego on the east.

The City of National City is a general law city with a
directly-elected mayor and four councilmembers. The current
City Council is comprised of Mayor George H. Waters, Vice-
Mayor Ralph Inzunza, Councilman Michael R. Dalla, Councilman
Jessie E. Van Deventer and Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate.

Pursuant to the Community Redevelopment Law, Health and Safety
Code Section 33000 et. seq., there is in each community a
public body known as the redevelopment agency of the community
(Health and Safety Code Section 33100). The redevelopment
agency in each community is "activated" by enactment of an
ordinance by the legislative body declaring that there is a
need for an agency to function in the community (Section
33101). The Redevelopment Agency of the City of National City
was activated on April 11, 1967, pursuant to Ordinance No.
1167. On October 14, 1975, the Community Development Commission

February 8, 1991
Page 2


sion of the City of National City, a combined redevelopment
agency and housing authority, was formed pursuant to Ordinance
No. 1484. Also pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484, the City
Council of the city of National City declared itself to be the
Community Development Commission of the City of National City.

Beginning in 1969, a series of redevelopment projects were
adopted in the community, namely, the E. J. Christman Business
& Industrial Park I Redevelopment Project; the E. J. Christman
Business & Industrial Park Redevelopment Project, Amendment
No. II; the South Bay Town & Country Redevelopment Project;
and the Center City Redevelopment Project. These projects
were merged with additional land to form the National City
Downtown Redevelopment Project, pursuant to Ordinance No.
1762, adopted on December 1, 1981. Minor amendments to the
Redevelopment Plan, which added a total of approximately three
acres to the Project, were approved by Ordinance No. 1821,
adopted on May 22, 1984, (Amendment I), and by Ordinance No.
1851, adopted on April 16, 1985 (Amendment II). With the 1984
and 1985 amendments, the National City Downtown Redevelopment
Project consists of approximately 2,083 acres - approximately
44% of the City's land area.

A copy of the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project
Redevelopment Plan, (which includes as Exhibit "1" a Project
Area Boundary Map), is enclosed as Exhibit "A." The documents
pertaining to Amendments I and II of the Redevelopment Plan
are enclosed as Exhibit "B."

By letter of July 13, 1990, I requested a Commission Opinion
(eventually treated as a request for written advice) on the
propriety of Mayor Waters, then Vice-Mayor Van Deventer,
Councilman Dalla and former Councilman Pruitt in participating
in decisions to amend the National City Downtown Redevelopment
Project Redevelopment Plan (referred to as "Amendment III").
As of that time, Amendment III contemplated designation of new
"acquisition areas" within the existing redevelopment project
area, as well as the addition of territory to the project area
as another acquisition area. I received your written response
to my request on or about October 5, 1990 (your file No. I-90-
559). By separate letter, I am requesting reconsideration of
your advice letter.

At the present time, the redevelopment plan amendment de-
scribed in my previous request for advice has been "tabled."
A new redevelopment plan amendment, also known as "Amendment
III," is being considered. The new Amendment III is intended
to accomplish two primary objectives: (1) to amend the

February 8, 1991
Page 3


     duration of the redevelopment plan, the limit on tax alloca-
tions, and the limit on bonded indebtedness, of the existing
plan; (2) to allow payment by the CDC of part of the cost of
acquiring land for and construction of a new police facility.
A more detailed description of the objectives of the redevel-
opment plan amendment is set forth below.  A copy of the
report of the Executive Director of the Community Development
Commission on the proposed Amendment III is attached as
Exhibit "C." (It should be noted that the element of the
amendment pertaining to the Bonita Vista Mobilehome Park has
been deleted.)

II. PROPOSED AMENDMENTS TO NATIONAL CITY DOWNTOWN REDEVELOPMENT
    PLAN

    A.  Amendment of Duration of Plan, Limit on Total Allocation,
        Limit on Bonded Indebtedness.

        1.  Existing Plan

            The existing National City Downtown Redevelopment
            Project Redevelopment Plan contains, at pages 15
            through 16, provisions for the allocation of tax
            revenues pursuant to the terms of Health and Safety
            Code Section 33670.

            Pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 33333.2,
            the Redevelopment Plan provides, at page 16, that
            the number of dollars of taxes which may be divided
            and allocated to the CDC in connection with the
            plan is $58 Million.

            Pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 33334.1,
            the Redevelopment Plan provides, at page 16, that
            the amount of bonded indebtedness which can be
            outstanding at one time is limited at $18.2 Mil-
            lion.

            Pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 33333.2,
            the Redevelopment Plan provides, at page 16, that
            the duration on the establishment of loans, advanc-
            es, and indebtedness to finance the plan is through
            the year 2005.

        2.  Proposed Amendment

            The proposed Amendment III would provide for the
            following:

February 8, 1991
Page 4


a.    Increasing the number of dollars of taxes
      which may be divided and allocated from $58
      Million to $250 Million.

b.    Increasing the amount of bonded indebtedness
      which can be outstanding from $18.2 Million to
      $100 Million.

c.    Extending the duration of the provisions,
      requirements, and controls of the Redevelop-
      ment Plan from the year 2005 to 2015.

As stated in the Executive Director's report, the
financial objectives of the proposed amendment are
the following:

a.    Pay debt service on existing bonds ($1.7
      Million);

b.    Make payments to other taxing agencies, name-
      ly, the County of San Diego, the National
      School District, and the Sweetwater Union High
      School    District,    probably    through "pass-
      through" agreements (total approximate pay-
      ments in excess of $8 Million);

c.    20% set aside for low income housing;

d.    Payment of overhead and administrative expens-
      es;

e.    Allocate the remaining funds one-half to
      neighborhood improvement, and one-half to
      commercial and industrial development.

The proposed amendment does not designate any
specific sites within the redevelopment project
area for the expenditure of funds accruing from the
sale of bonds which would be made possible by the
amendment.  The proposed amendment would not result
in the addition of territory to the redevelopment
project area.

B.    Amendment to Allow Payment by CDC of Part of Cost of
      Acquiring Land for and Construction of a Police Facility.

Currently, nearly all administrative offices for the City
of National City are located in the National City Civic

February 8, 1991
Page 5


Center, located at 1243 National City Boulevard, National
City.  An exception exists in the case of the fire
department, which occupies two fire stations, both
located in buildings apart from the Civic Center. The
police department occupies approximately 15,000 square
feet in the Civic Center basement, and additionally
leases 4,270 square feet of office space in an office
building at 43 East 12th Street, immediately to the north
of the Civic Center. The Civic Center itself, built in
1964, is approximately 41,000 square feet in area.

Located in the vicinity of the Civic Center are a
community recreation building known as Kimball Community
Building, located at 140 East 12th Street; the National
City Public Library, located at 200 East 12th Street; and
Kimball Park, a 27 acre public park located immediately
behind the Civic Center and extending to "D" Street.

A map depicting the above facilities is attached hereto
as Exhibit "D."

In 1984, a study was completed by an architecture and
planning consultant, which concluded that the National
City Police Department operates out of a facility which
is "substantially inadequate" in terms of space needs and
amenities.  Since that time, various alternatives have
been considered by the City Council to remedy this
situation, including construction of a new Civic Center.
However, the City Council has recently determined to
construct a new police facility only.  Although a final
determination of the size of the police facility is
awaiting a space needs assessment by a private consul-
tant, it is currently estimated that the size of the
facility will be 50,000 to 60,000 square feet in area.

The City Council has also recently determined that the
new police facility should be constructed on a 57,494
square foot site on National City Boulevard, directly
across from the existing Civic Center. The site consists
of seven parcels, occupied by three residences, a plasma
center, an auto upholstery business, and two used car
lots.  A map depicting the site, together with explanato-
ry materials, are attached hereto collectively as Exhibit
"E."

National City Boulevard, on which the existing Civic
Center as well as the proposed site for the new police
facility is located, is a major commercial "strip" in

February 8, 1991
Page 6


National City, heavily-developed with new and used
automobile dealerships and related automotive businesses.
Four blocks to the north of the Civic Center and the
proposed new police facility site is Acquisition Area "A"
of the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project.
Recent redevelopment activity in Acquisition Area "A"
consists of the acquisition of privately-owned property
and sale to a redeveloper for construction of two hotels
and a parking garage, with tentative plans for a future
office building.

The Community Redevelopment Law, at Section 33445 of the
California Health and Safety Code, provides that if
certain findings are made by the legislative body of the
community, the redevelopment agency may pay all or part
of the cost of the value of the land for, and the cost of
the installation and construction of, any building,
facility, structure, or other improvement which is
publicly owned, either within or without the project
area. At the present time, consideration is being given
to a proposal whereby the Community Development Commission of the City of National City would pay for part of
the cost of the value of land and of construction of the
building to be constructed for the new police facility.
Due to the requirements of Section 33445, it is necessary
that the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project
Redevelopment Plan be amended to specifically provide for
the partial payment by the CDC of the land and building
for the police facility. The amendment would not result
in the addition of territory to the redevelopment project
area.

III. FINANCIAL INTERESTS OF NATIONAL CITY COUNCILMEMBERS

A.    Mayor George H. Waters

Mayor George H. Waters owns the following interests
within the City of National City, and within the boundaries of the Project Area of the National City Downtown
Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan. These interests were acquired approximately 25 years ago:

•     A fee interest in real property located at 1436
Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
property is greater than $100,000. The property is
improved with a commercial building, which is
rented to Dr. Rudolfo Picazo, a dentist, producing
an annual income to Mr. Waters of between $1,000

February 8, 1991
Page 7


                    and $10,000.  The lease to Dr. Picazo ends on
                December 31, 1991, and is renewable for an addi-
                tional one year term upon mutual agreement.

        •   A fee interest in real property located at 1440
            Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
            property is greater than $100,000.  The property is
            improved with a residence, which is rented, produc-
            ing annual income of between $1,000 and $10,000.

        •   A fee interest in real property located at 1442
            Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
            property is greater than $100,000.  The property is
            improved with a commercial building, which is
            rented to Maniflo Money Exchange, a money exchange
            which buys and sells foreign currency, producing
            annual income to Mr. Waters of between $1,000 and
            $10,000.  The lease to Maniflo Money Exchange ends
            on December 31, 1991, and is renewable for an
            additional one year term upon mutual agreement.

        •   A fee interest in real property located at 1444-
            1446 Highland Avenue.  The fair market value of the
            property is greater than $100,000.  The property is
            improved with a commercial building, which is used
            as the place of business for a commercial office
            machines sales and service business of which Mayor
            Waters is the proprietor.

        None of Mayor Waters' properties is located within 2,500
        feet of the proposed new police facility site.

B.     Councilman Michael R. Dalla

       Councilman Michael R. Dalla owns a personal residence at
       1020 "C" Avenue, acquired in 1974, which is within the
       City of National City, and within the boundaries of the
       Redevelopment Project Area.  This property is located
       beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the proposed
       police facility site.

C.     Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer

       Councilman Jessie E. Van Deventer owns the following
       interests within the City of National City, and within
       the boundaries of the Project Area of the National City
       Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment Plan. These

February 8, 1991
Page 8


     interests were acquired from the early 1960's through
1987.

     •    A fee interest in real property at 110 East 16th
Street.  The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000.  The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to
Thompson Lacquer, a retailer of auto finish prod-
ucts, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.  The term of the lease is
from February 15, 1989 to February 14, 1995.

     •    A fee interest in real property at 34 East 17th
Street.  The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000.  The property is improved
with a commercial building.  Suite A is rented to
Manuel Acuna, an import auto parts store, producing
an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of
$10,000.  The term of the lease is from November 1,
1990 to October 31, 2000.  Suites B and C are
rented to Alba Rodriguez, who operates a party
rental business and an auto body and repair business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.  The term of the lease is
from October 1, 1990 to September 30, 1992.

     •    A fee interest in real property at 106 East 17th
Street.  The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000.  The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to
Dobson Welding, a welding shop, producing an annual
income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.
The term of the lease is from October 1, 1990 to
September 30, 2000.

     •    A fee interest in real property at 25 East 17th
Street.  The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000.  The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to Bush
Power Brake Service, a truck and automotive brake
repair business, producing an annual income to Mr.
Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.  The term of the
lease is from February 15, 1985 to February 14,
1995.

     •    A fee interest in real property at 115 East 17th
Street.  The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000.  The property is improved

February 8, 1991
Page 9


with a commercial building. Suite A is rented to
Brake World, a truck and automotive brake repair
business, producing an annual income to Mr. Van
Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the
lease is from April 15, 1990 to April 14, 1993.
Suite B is rented to Safelite Glass, an auto glass
service and repair business, producing an annual
income to Mr. Van Deventer in excess of $10,000.
The term of the lease is from September 1, 1988 to
August 31, 1993. Suite C is rented to Trini's Auto
Electric, an auto electric repair business, produc-
ing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of approx-
imately $10,000. The term of the lease is from
September 15, 1988 to September 14, 1993. Suite D
is rented to Ted's Upholstery, an upholstery shop,
producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer of
approximately $10,000. The term of the lease is
from February 1, 1990 to January 31, 1993.

     •  A fee interest in real property at 125 East 17th
Street, improved with a commercial structure and
having a fair market value of over $100,000. This
property is used as the place of business for
Vancon Corporation, a closely held corporation of
which Councilman Van Deventer is the majority
stockholder. The property is leased to Vancon
Corporation, generating income to Mr. Van Deventer
of approximately $5,000 annually. The term of the
lease is from July 1, 1989 to June 30, 1992. A
portion of this property is also leased to Marin
Income Tax Service, an income tax service, on a
month to month basis, producing an annual income to
Mr. Van Deventer of approximately $5,000.

     •  A fee interest in real property at 41 East 18th
Street. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to J.D.
Brothers Auto Repair, an auto repair business,
producing an annual income to Mr. Van Deventer in
excess of $10,000. The term of the lease is from
February 1, 1988 to December 15, 1991.

     •  A fee interest in real property at 115 East 18th
Street. The fair market value of the property is
greater than $100,000. The property is improved
with a commercial building, which is rented to
Prochem Specialty Products, a wholesaler of clean-

February 8, 1991
Page 10


ing solutions, producing an annual income to Mr.
Van Deventer in excess of $10,000. The term of the
lease is from November 1, 1990 to October 31, 1993.

Councilman van Deventer's properties are located beyond
300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the proposed new police
facility site.

In addition to the above real property interests,
Councilman Van Deventer has the following financial
interests:

•  During 1989 Wirtz Tile Company, Inc. was granted
   authority to draw upon a line of credit held in the
   name of Mr. Van Deventer and his wife at San Diego
   National Bank.  The draw against said line of
   credit was approximately $75,000.00 in total during
   1989.  Wirtz Tile makes all payments due to San
   Diego National Bank stemming from said draw.  Mr.
   Van Deventer's interest in the Wirtz business is
   solely beneficial and as security for said draw.
   The total consideration paid to Mr. Van Deventer
   from Wirtz Tile Company, Inc. was $2,000. Wirtz
   Tile Company, Inc. is located at 1533 Wilson Ave-
   nue, National City, within the redevelopment pro-
   ject area but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new
   police facility site.

•  Mr. Van Deventer is the beneficiary of a note with
   a current balance of approximately $5,000, secured
   by a second deed of trust on a residence located at
   2965 Ridgeway Drive, National City, which is out-
   side the boundaries of the redevelopment project
   and beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new police
   facility site. The trustor under the deed of trust
   and obligor on the note is Juan Lopez, who owns an
   automotive machine shop at 1526 East 18th Street,
   National City, located within the redevelopment
   project area but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed
   new police facility site.  Mr. Van Deventer re-
   ceives a monthly payment of $108 under the note.

•  Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory
   note in the amount of $52,178.56, executed by Nancy
   Thompson, owner of three child care facilities in
   National City.  The child care facilities are
   located at 2207 Highland Avenue, 2200 "E" Avenue,
   and 1540 Harbison Avenue, National City.  The

February 8, 1991
Page 11


                    Highland Avenue and "E" Avenue locations are within
                    the boundaries of the redevelopment project area,
                    but beyond 2,500 feet of the proposed new police
                    facility site.  The Harbison Avenue location is
                    outside the redevelopment project area and beyond
                    2,500 feet of the proposed new police facility
                    site.    Mr. Van Deventer received approximately
                    $3,500 in payment of this note in 1990.

                •   Mr. Van Deventer is the obligee under a promissory
                    note in the amount of $11,889.55, executed by
                    Eugenio Trinidad, also a lessee of Mr. Van
                    Deventer's and the operator of an auto electric
                    repair business at 115 East 17th Street, Suite C,
                    National City. This location is within the rede-
                    velopment project area, and beyond 300 feet but
                    within 2,500 feet of the proposed new police facil-
                    ity site. This note is secured by the inventory of
                    Mr. Trinidad's business. Mr. Van Deventer received
                    $753.61 in income under this note in 1990.

D.     Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate

       Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate owns the following
       interests in real property within the City of National
       City, and within the boundaries of the Project Area of
       the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project
       Redevelopment Plan:

       •   A fee interest in real property at 1735 Plaza
           Boulevard, acquired in 1978. The fair market value
           of the property is greater than $100,000.  The
           property is improved with an apartment building,
           which is rented to individual tenants on month-to-
           month tenancies, producing an annual income of over
           $10,000.

       •   A personal residence located at 1730 East 12th
           Street, acquired in 1964.

       Neither of Councilwoman Zarate's properties is located
       within 2,500 feet of the proposed new police facility
       site.

February 8, 1991
Page 12


IV.  PUBLIC GENERALLY EXCEPTION

     Commission Regulation 18703 provides that the "public general-
ly" exception will apply to the financial interests of an
official if a decision affects the official's interests in
substantially the same manner as it will affect a significant
segment of the public. It appears that statistical information
regarding the number of owners of residential properties,
and of commercial real estate and business interests in the
redevelopment project area, is significant in determining if
the public generally exception applies. Accordingly, I have
requested such information from the City Planning and Finance
Departments. The findings of these departments are set forth
hereinbelow:

     A.   Number of Commercial and Industrial Buildings within the
          Redevelopment Project Area.

          Based on a survey completed at my request by the National
          City Planning Department, it was determined that 712
          commercial and industrial buildings are located in the
          redevelopment project area. A copy of the memorandum
          from the Planning Department containing this determina-
          tion is attached hereto as Exhibit "F."

     B.   Number of Residential Buildings within the Redevelopment
          Project Area.

          A Planning Department survey of residential structures
          indicates that there are 6,776 single family residences,
          and 8,280 multiple family residences within the City of
          National City. Of these, it is estimated that approxima-
          tely 4,525 single family residences and 5,550 multi-
          family units are within the redevelopment project area.
          A copy of the memorandum from the Planning Department
          containing this determination is attached hereto as
          Exhibit "G."

     C.   Number of Businesses with Sales Tax Permits in the
          Redevelopment Project Area.

          In order to further substantiate the number of business
          interests within the redevelopment project area, I
          requested and received a study from the National City
          Finance Department on the number of businesses with sales
          tax permits within the redevelopment project area. The
          Finance Department has determined that there are 1,782
          businesses with sales tax permits located in the project
          area.

February 8, 1991
Page 13


area.  This number represents 78% of all businesses with
sales tax permits in the City.  A copy of the memorandum
from the Finance Department setting forth the information
with regard to sales tax permits is attached hereto as
Exhibit "H."

V.  ISSUES PRESENTED

Are Mayor Waters, Councilman Van Deventer, or Councilwoman
Zarate precluded from participating in action(s) to amend the
National City Downtown Redevelopment Project Redevelopment
Plan, by virtue of the financial interests disclosed above,
and in view of the provisions of Section 18702(c) (the "Public
Generally Exception"), of the Regulations of the Fair Politi-
cal Practices Commission?

Very truly yours,

[signature]

George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney

GHE/sbc

Pursuant to the Community Redevelopment Law, Health and Safety Code Section 33000 et. seq., there is in each community a public body known as the redevelopment agency of the community (Health and Safety Code Section 33100). The redevelopment agency in each community is "activated" by enactment of an ordinance by the legislative body declaring that there is a need for an agency to function in the community (Section 33101). The Redevelopment Agency of the City of National City was activated on April 11, 1967, pursuant to Ordinance No. 1167. On October 14, 1975, the Community Development Commission February 8, 1991 Page 2 sion of the City of National City, a combined redevelopment agency and housing authority, was formed pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484. Also pursuant to Ordinance No. 1484, the City Council of the city of National City declared itself to be the Community Development Commission of the City of National City. Beginning in 1969, a series of redevelopment projects were adopted in the community, namely, the E. J. Christman Business & Industrial Park I Redevelopment Project; the E. J. Christman Business & Industrial Park Redevelopment Project, Amendment No. II; the South Bay Town & Country Redevelopment Project; and the Center City Redevelopment Project. These projects were merged with additional land to form the National City Downtown Redevelopment Project, pursuant to Ordinance No. 1762, adopted on December 1, 1981.
